questions.stem: A dataset where 95% of examples are class A and 5% are class B is described as:
options: Noisy
Imbalanced
Unlabelled
Contaminated
questions.explanation: Class imbalance occurs when one class significantly outnumbers others. This causes models to learn to predict the majority class, achieving high accuracy but failing on the minority class.

questions.stem: Data leakage in a machine learning pipeline means:
options: Your training data is publicly visible
Features from the test set were used during training, giving artificially high evaluation scores
The model's weights were accidentally shared
Missing values in the dataset
questions.explanation: Data leakage is when information about the test (or future) data influences training, making evaluation scores unrealistically high. The model appears to work but fails in production.

questions.stem: Which is an example of data annotation bias?
options: Training on images from only one country, leading to poor global performance
Using too few training examples
Not normalising input features
Setting the learning rate too high
questions.explanation: Annotation bias occurs when training data doesn't represent the full real-world distribution — e.g. a face recognition model trained only on images from one demographic group performs poorly on others.

questions.stem: Why should you never use the test set for model selection or hyperparameter tuning?
options: Test sets are too small for tuning
It makes training slower
Repeatedly evaluating on the test set leaks information and makes the test score an overestimate of true performance
Test sets don't contain labels
questions.explanation: The test set must be used only once — for final evaluation. Using it for model selection is a form of leakage: you implicitly tune to it, and reported performance won't generalise to new data.

questions.stem: What is label noise in a training dataset?
options: Audio noise in speech datasets
Some training examples have incorrect labels, which can teach the model wrong patterns
Features with high variance
Missing values in the target column
questions.explanation: Label noise means some training examples are mislabelled. This degrades model quality because the model learns from incorrect supervision. Noisy labels are common in crowd-sourced annotation datasets.

options: 80% is always the right amount of training data
Train teaches the model, validation guides model selection and tuning, test provides unbiased final evaluation
It's required by sklearn's API
More data is always better regardless of the split
questions.explanation: The three-way split separates concerns: train for learning, validation for iterative decisions (hyperparameters, architecture), test for honest final evaluation. Using only two splits risks contaminating the test signal.
